# Source Card: Diagnostic utility of protein to creatinine ratio (P/C ratio) in spot urine sample within routine clinical practice

## Summary
This 2020 peer-reviewed narrative review (Critical Reviews in Clinical Laboratory Sciences) synthesizes the evidence for spot (random) urine protein-to-creatinine ratio as a fast substitute for 24-hour urine protein quantification across kidney disease, hypertension, pregnancy/preeclampsia, immunological disease, diabetes, myeloma, and children. For the pediatric CDS module it establishes the commonly-reported general cutoffs (proteinuria >20 mg/mmol; nephrotic >350 mg/mmol) and the ISSHP obstetric cutoff (30 mg/mmol), and it explicitly warns that the correct cutoff is population- and laboratory-method-dependent (Jaffe vs enzymatic creatinine). It does NOT, in the accessible passage, provide a pediatric-specific numeric threshold — that is recorded as a gap.

## Key evidence (with exact-passage locators)
- (ev_001) General proteinuria-detection cutoff most commonly reported as >20 mg/mmol (0.2 mg/mg) — locator: Abstract, results sentence 1 — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:22a13e59ea622a97d1e3c739e07f1a91572e82775f5ac17eba0031adcc8f7ece]"
- (ev_002) Nephrotic-range confirmation at >350 mg/mmol (3.5 mg/mg) — locator: Abstract, results sentence 1 — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:2f42c2709e7ceb8370c6262a2e96e9c7202a98e3f69279ec60f479fa061aa3bf]"
- (ev_003) ISSHP obstetric cutoff 30 mg/mmol (0.3 mg/mg) for preeclampsia-risk classification — locator: Abstract, results sentence 2 — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:d5a6ec5cc77f660ab4136ff412bb52ebf58826df08d91eeddb0fb8725e32c320]"
- (ev_004) Cutoff must be set per lab; depends on population and methodology — locator: Abstract, closing recommendation — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:b6bf2d5efdf83e6cc5864e6036a4d0fc115ffacbe6c6fb1b716129de1a108dc2]"
- (ev_005) High correlation of spot P/C with 24-h protein — locator: Abstract, correlation sentence — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:0f15edd1eedbcd886318f21025b1f501eecba49a2aee19ac1ce7feec723fb221]"

## Population / assay / lifecycle notes
- Populations covered by the review: adults with kidney disease/hypertension/diabetes/myeloma/immunological disease, pregnant women (preeclampsia risk), and children (a dedicated section). The three numeric cutoffs captured here (20, 350, 30 mg/mmol) are stated as general or obstetric, NOT as pediatric-derived reference values.
- Assay/method dependence is a first-class caveat in this source: the calculated ratio and its cutoff depend on the urine creatinine method (Jaffe vs enzymatic, named in the article keywords) and the urine protein method. Units used throughout are mg/mmol with mg/mg equivalents.
- Lifecycle: 2020 review; cites ISSHP for the pregnancy cutoff. No effective/retire dates given for the individual cutoffs.

## Scope-exit / referral relevance (if any)
- Indirect: nephrotic-range P/C (>350 mg/mmol) marks a high-severity proteinuria band that a CDS could treat as an escalation/red-flag threshold. The source frames it diagnostically ("confirms nephrotic proteinuria"), not as an executable referral directive.

## Limitations & conflicts
- GAP — pediatric-specific threshold not locatable in source 32058809 (accessible passage): the review names a children's proteinuria section but the paywalled full text (HTTP 403) was not retrievable, and the structured abstract contains no pediatric-specific P/C numeric cutoff or age band. Do NOT apply the general adult/obstetric cutoffs to children without a pediatric primary source.
- Cutoff conflict PRESERVED (not averaged): proteinuria-detection cutoff 20 mg/mmol (ev_001) vs ISSHP obstetric cutoff 30 mg/mmol (ev_003) reflect different populations and definitions; they are distinct thresholds, not a range to be collapsed.
- Method-dependence caveat (ev_004) means any single numeric cutoff is not portable across analyzers/populations; a CDS rule derived from this source must carry the Jaffe-vs-enzymatic and population qualifier.
- Source is a secondary narrative review (not a primary diagnostic-accuracy or reference-interval study); numeric cutoffs are "most commonly reported" summaries, not the review authors' own derived values.
